summaries: Between {from_time} and {to_time} UTC on {date}, customers using {service} in the us-east-1 Region may have experienced elevated error rates and gateway timeouts. Investigation identified that {deep_downstream} began responding at {latency}ms following a deployment at {deploy_time} UTC, causing {downstream} to hold connections open until its {downstream_timeout}s read timeout fired. This exhausted {service}'s connection pool {pool_name} ({thread_max} threads), producing HTTP 504 responses for all callers. Reverting the {deep_downstream} deployment restored baseline latency.

2:17 PM PST We are investigating elevated 504 error rates for {service} in us-west-2. 2:24 PM PST Distributed tracing confirms the latency originates at {deep_downstream}, which is responding at {latency}ms. {downstream} is holding connections open for {downstream_timeout}s before timing out. {service} connection pool {pool_name} is exhausted. 2:31 PM PST The {deep_downstream} timeout has been reduced to 2s as an interim mitigation. Pool utilisation is decreasing. 2:38 PM PST The issue with {service} has been resolved. All pools have returned to normal.

The issue affecting {service} in the West Europe Region has been mitigated as of {resolved_time} UTC. Between {from_time} and {to_time} UTC, customers may have experienced HTTP 504 errors. P99 latency was pinned at exactly {timeout}s — the hard-ceiling pattern of upstream timeout propagation. Root cause was elevated response time at {deep_downstream} ({latency}ms), which caused {downstream} to hold connections open for its full {downstream_timeout}s read timeout, subsequently exhausting {service}'s {pool_name} pool.

Post Incident Review – {service} Gateway Timeout Event. Tracking ID: SRE-{incident_id}. What happened? Between {from_time} UTC and {to_time} UTC, {service} returned HTTP 504 to {error_pct}% of requests. Request rate was unchanged at {rate} RPS throughout — this was not a volume event. Each in-flight request occupied a thread in {pool_name} for the full {timeout}s duration while waiting for {downstream}, which was itself blocked on {deep_downstream}. Pool capacity of {thread_max} threads was reached within 90 seconds of {deep_downstream} slowing. Resolution: rollback of {deep_downstream} at {resolved_time} UTC.

9:17 AM UTC {service} p99 latency has jumped to {timeout}s. Thread dump shows all {active} active threads blocked in SocketInputStream.read() waiting for {downstream}. No increase in inbound request rate — {rate} RPS is normal. 9:19 AM UTC Tracing confirms the latency wall originates at {deep_downstream} (responding at {latency}ms). {downstream} pool {pool_name} is also saturated. 9:23 AM UTC Read timeout on {downstream} → {deep_downstream} reduced to 2s. {service} pool utilisation dropping. 9:31 AM UTC Incident resolved.

Customers using {service} may have experienced intermittent gateway timeouts between {from_time} and {to_time} UTC. The failure mode was thread-pool exhaustion at {service}: each request to {service} required a call to {downstream}, which was slow due to degradation at {deep_downstream}. Because {service}'s configured timeout for {downstream} ({timeout}s) exceeded {downstream}'s own timeout for {deep_downstream} ({downstream_timeout}s), {service} was able to accumulate {active} blocked threads before any shedding occurred. Arithmetic: {rate} RPS × {timeout}s = {active} concurrent threads, matching the {thread_max} pool ceiling exactly.

4:32 PM SGT {service} health checks began failing after {active} concurrent requests accumulated in pool {pool_name}. Distributed trace analysis shows each request span ends with a SocketTimeoutException after exactly {timeout}s, all blocking on a downstream call to {downstream}. {downstream} traces show identical saturation, with all threads blocked on {deep_downstream}. No retry amplification observed — request rates are 1:1 at each hop. This is pure hold-time propagation.

Between {from_time} UTC and {to_time} UTC, {service} in ap-northeast-1 experienced a full-pool saturation event that produced {error_pct}% HTTP 504 responses. Inbound request volume was stable at {rate} RPS. The root cause was a slow database query introduced in the {deploy_time} UTC deployment of {deep_downstream}, which raised query execution time to {latency}ms. {downstream} exhausted its connection pool holding {conn_count} sockets open. {service} followed within {timeout}s. The propagation sequence took approximately {timeout}s from first {deep_downstream} slowdown to full {service} unavailability.

We believe the {service} gateway timeout event in us-central1 has been resolved for all affected customers as of {resolved_time} US/Pacific. The incident was caused by a slow dependency two hops away, not by any change to {service} itself. {deep_downstream} introduced elevated query latency at {deploy_time} UTC; {downstream} held connections open for its {downstream_timeout}s read timeout; {service} held connections to {downstream} open for its {timeout}s timeout. The {service} thread pool reached its {thread_max}-thread ceiling within {timeout}s of {deep_downstream} slowing.

11:48 AM UTC Alert: {service} — {timeout_pct}% of requests are breaching the {timeout}s SLO threshold simultaneously. The pattern is a vertical cliff, not a ramp. This is not consistent with load-induced slowdown. Distributed tracing shows all slow requests waiting on {downstream}, which is waiting on {deep_downstream}. {deep_downstream} is responding slowly but not erroring, which is the signature of a timeout-driven saturation event propagating upstream. Recommended action: reduce {downstream} timeout to break the hold chain.

Summary of impact: Between {from_time} and {to_time} UTC on {date}, {service} in the eu-central-1 Region experienced elevated 504 error rates. Circuit breaker for {service} → {downstream} did not trip during the early propagation phase: requests were slow but not yet failing, allowing pool {pool_name} to fill before the error-rate threshold was reached. By the time the circuit breaker tripped to OPEN, {active} threads were already blocked. Rollback of {deep_downstream} resolved the underlying latency within {downstream_timeout}s.

7:03 AM EST We are investigating elevated error rates for {service}. Connection pool {pool_name} shows {conn_count} connections in ESTABLISHED state with zero bytes read in the last {timeout}s — all waiting on {downstream}. New requests are queuing; once {waiting} exceeds {thread_max} the service will begin shedding with HTTP 503. No increase in inbound request rate — {rate} RPS is within normal range. This is a hold-time saturation event caused by a slow upstream dependency.

The {service} incident on {date} was not caused by increased load. Inbound rate was {rate} RPS throughout — identical to the baseline. The saturation of pool {pool_name} is explained entirely by connection hold time increasing from <200ms to {timeout}s. At {rate} RPS, a {timeout}s hold time requires {active} concurrent threads, exactly equal to the {thread_max} pool ceiling. The hold time increase was caused by {deep_downstream} responding at {latency}ms following a slow query deployment. No scaling action would have resolved this event.

Between {from_time} and {to_time} UTC, all tiers in the {service} call chain reported latency at their respective configured timeout values: {timeout}s at {service}, {downstream_timeout}s at {downstream}, {total_timeout}s at the edge. This staircase of hard ceilings is the definitive fingerprint of synchronous timeout propagation through a blocking I/O call chain. The slow component was {deep_downstream}; all upstream tiers failed by exhausting their thread pools waiting for the tier below them.

10:22 PM UTC Incident update for {service}: we have identified the propagation chain. At {from_time} UTC, {deep_downstream} began responding at {latency}ms. By T+{downstream_timeout}s, {downstream} had exhausted pool {pool_name} holding {conn_count} open sockets. By T+{timeout}s, {service} was fully saturated with {active} threads blocked. Edge callers began receiving {total_timeout}s timeouts by T+{total_timeout}s. Mitigation applied: {downstream} timeout reduced from {downstream_timeout}s to 2s, releasing blocked threads progressively.

The {service} p99 latency spike to {timeout}s on {date} was not a network or application regression at {service} itself — no deployments had occurred within four hours. Tracing confirmed all slow requests were blocked on {downstream}, which was blocked on {deep_downstream}. A slow database query introduced in the {deep_downstream} deployment at {deploy_time} UTC was the root cause. Rolling back {deep_downstream} restored query execution time to baseline and cleared the hold chain within one minute.

Blocking I/O thread-hold analysis for {service}: each slow response from {downstream} occupies exactly one thread for exactly {timeout}s. No retries are issued. The saturation is linear: {rate} RPS inbound × {timeout}s hold time per request = {active} threads required. Pool ceiling is {thread_max}. No amount of horizontal scaling at {service} would resolve this — the correct fix is reducing the timeout for {downstream} to a value shorter than {downstream}'s own timeout for {deep_downstream}, ensuring {service} sheds load before pool exhaustion.

Post-incident: the timeout configuration across {service}, {downstream}, and {deep_downstream} was uncoordinated. {service} was configured to wait {timeout}s for {downstream}; {downstream} was configured to wait {downstream_timeout}s for {deep_downstream}. The correct configuration is a descending timeout ladder: {deep_downstream} read timeout < {downstream} timeout < {service} timeout < edge timeout, with each tier shedding before the tier above exhausts. Recommended values: {deep_downstream} 1s → {downstream} 3s → {service} 5s → edge 8s.

3:55 AM UTC SRE on-call engaged for {service} — {error_pct}% error rate, all HTTP 504. Thread dump: {active} threads in WAITING state, all blocked in SocketInputStream.read() on connection to {downstream}. Stack trace consistent across all {active} threads — this is not a code path issue, it is a dependency hold issue. {downstream} shows the same pattern against {deep_downstream}. Mitigation: circuit breaker on {service} → {downstream} manually tripped to OPEN; fast-fail responses released pool threads. {deep_downstream} rollback initiated in parallel.

Preliminary root cause: {service} gateway timeouts on {date} were caused by synchronous dependency saturation, not by any fault in {service} itself. {deep_downstream} experienced a query regression at {deploy_time} UTC, responding at {latency}ms. Because connections to {downstream} are held open under blocking I/O until the {timeout}s timeout fires, and because {rate} RPS × {timeout}s exceeds the {thread_max}-thread pool ceiling, the service entered full saturation within {timeout}s of the {deep_downstream} regression onset. Circuit breaker configuration did not prevent saturation because slow requests do not register as errors until the timeout fires.

messages: CRITICAL [{service}] Thread pool {pool_name} exhausted: {active}/{thread_max} threads blocked on {downstream} I/O (hold_time~{timeout}s) | {waiting} requests queued | p99={timeout}s | HTTP 504 being returned to callers

CRITICAL [{service}] 100% of sampled requests timing out — p99 latency = {timeout}s (hard ceiling) | all threads waiting on {downstream} | pool={pool_name} | downstream trace confirms saturation at {deep_downstream}

CRITICAL [{service}] Thread starvation: {waiting} requests queued, {active} threads in blocking wait for {downstream} | new requests will be rejected with HTTP 503 | inbound rate {rate} RPS unchanged — this is a hold-time event

CRITICAL [{service}] Error rate {error_pct}% — all failures are SocketTimeoutException after exactly {timeout}s | upstream edge latency now at {total_timeout}s | pool {pool_name} at ceiling {thread_max}

CRITICAL [{service}] Full call-chain saturation confirmed: {deep_downstream} → {downstream} → {service} → edge all reporting latency at respective timeout ceilings | {timeout}s | {downstream_timeout}s | {total_timeout}s

CRITICAL [{service}] {conn_count} open sockets to {downstream} — zero bytes received | pool {pool_name} exhausted | service effectively unavailable | {affected_count} downstream clients impacted | reduce {downstream} timeout immediately

CRITICAL [{service}] SLO breach: {timeout_pct}% of requests exceeding {timeout}s | cause: {downstream} holding connections pending slow response from {deep_downstream} | vertical latency cliff (not gradual) — timeout propagation pattern

CRITICAL [{pool_name}] Queue depth {waiting} exceeded pool ceiling {thread_max} | {service} shedding HTTP 503 | {affected_count} clients affected | fix: reduce {downstream} timeout or roll back {deep_downstream}

CRITICAL [{service}] Timeout arithmetic: {rate} RPS × {timeout}s hold time = {active} concurrent threads required = {thread_max} pool ceiling | any additional load will queue indefinitely | circuit breaker not yet tripped

CRITICAL [{service}] Coordinated timeout event: {downstream} connection hold time increased from <100ms to {downstream_timeout}s | {service} pool saturated within {timeout}s of {deep_downstream} slowdown | rollback {deep_downstream} to resolve
WARNING [{service}] p95 latency crossed {timeout}s threshold — {timeout_pct}% of requests at or near timeout ceiling | pool {pool_name} at {active}/{thread_max} | {downstream} response time degraded
WARNING [{service}] {downstream} response time increasing: current P50={latency}ms | if trend continues, {service} will saturate {pool_name} within ~3 minutes | no increase in inbound request rate
WARNING [{service}] Connection hold time to {downstream} elevated: {conn_count} connections open >10s (normal <200ms) | possible timeout propagation from {deep_downstream} in progress
WARNING [{pool_name}] Utilisation at {timeout_pct}% ({active}/{thread_max}) | headroom insufficient for traffic variations | {downstream} response degradation suspected | investigation in progress
WARNING [{downstream}] Response latency {latency}ms, approaching configured timeout {downstream_timeout}s | upstream caller {service} may begin accumulating blocked threads if not resolved

WARNING [{service}] Timeout budget mismatch: {service} timeout for {downstream} = {timeout}s | {downstream} timeout for {deep_downstream} = {downstream_timeout}s | if {deep_downstream} exceeds {downstream_timeout}s, propagation chain is possible

WARNING [{service}] Queue depth {waiting} requests waiting for {pool_name} thread | service degraded but not yet failing | latency increasing toward {timeout}s ceiling | {downstream} response time: {latency}ms

WARNING [{service}] Circuit breaker for {downstream} approaching trip threshold — error_rate={error_pct}% | slow requests not yet counted as errors | effective trip may lag pool saturation by ~{timeout}s
WARNING [{deep_downstream}] Query latency spike to {latency}ms | upstream callers {downstream} ({downstream_timeout}s timeout) and {service} ({timeout}s timeout) may be impacted if latency persists

WARNING [{service}] Blocked-thread count growing at rate correlated with {rate} RPS inbound | each request occupying thread for ~{timeout}s | pool exhaustion projected in <2 minutes without intervention

INFO [{service}] Timeout configuration audit initiated | {service} timeout for {downstream}: {timeout}s | {downstream} timeout for {deep_downstream}: {downstream_timeout}s | mismatch allows pool saturation before downstream sheds

INFO [{service}] Mitigation applied: {downstream} timeout reduced from {downstream_timeout}s to 2s | existing in-flight requests will still expire at {downstream_timeout}s | new requests will fast-fail | pool utilisation decreasing
INFO [{service}] Pool {pool_name} returning to normal: {active} active threads (was {thread_max}) | {waiting} queued (was {waiting}) | {deep_downstream} latency restored to baseline after rollback
INFO [{service}] Post-incident timeout ladder review scheduled | recommended config: {deep_downstream} 1s → {downstream} 3s → {service} 5s → edge 8s (staggered for progressive shedding at each tier)
INFO [{service}] Circuit breaker for {downstream} tripped to OPEN | fast-fail for 30s cooldown | pool {pool_name} threads releasing | cached or degraded responses being served where available
ERROR [{service}] RequestHandler-{active}: Read timed out after {timeout}s waiting for response from {downstream}:8080 — socket still ESTABLISHED, remote did not close | returning HTTP 504 to caller
ERROR [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Cannot acquire thread — pool exhausted ({active}/{thread_max} active, {waiting} queued) | rejecting inbound request | downstream: {downstream}
ERROR [{service}] TraceId={trace_id}: SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out (configured: {timeout}000ms) on connection to {downstream}/api/v1/query | upstream caller will receive HTTP 504

ERROR [{downstream}] worker-14: Timeout waiting for {deep_downstream} after {downstream_timeout}s | underlying connection remains open — remote alive but unresponsive | propagating timeout to caller {service}
ERROR [{service}] pool-monitor: Pool {pool_name} at {thread_max}/{thread_max} for {timeout}s — {waiting} callers queued | no threads returned in last 30s | all likely blocked on {downstream} I/O

ERROR [{service}] HealthCheck: Readiness probe failed — pool {pool_name} exhausted ({active} active, {waiting} waiting > threshold 50) | marking instance unhealthy | removing from load balancer rotation
ERROR [{service}] TraceId={trace_id}: Timeout propagated from {downstream} (elapsed: {downstream_timeout}s) + overhead = {timeout}s total | chain: {deep_downstream} → {downstream} → {service}
ERROR [{downstream}] conn-pool={pool_name}: {conn_count} connections to {deep_downstream} in state ESTABLISHED with 0 bytes received | all threads blocked | cannot accept new work from {service}
ERROR [{service}] RequestFilter: Rejected {affected_count} requests in last 10s due to pool {pool_name} exhaustion | all active threads blocked on {downstream} I/O | no retries issued

ERROR [{service}] TraceId={trace_id}: java.net.SocketTimeoutException — pool had no available connections after {timeout}s wait | increase pool size OR reduce downstream latency | downstream={downstream}

ERROR [{service}] MetricsExporter: SLO violation — {timeout_pct}% of requests exceeded {timeout}s budget in last 60s | all violations attributable to blocked I/O on {downstream} | error budget depleted

ERROR [{downstream}] db-client: PreparedStatement execution exceeded read timeout {downstream_timeout}s on {deep_downstream} | statement not cancelled | connection held open | all pool threads now occupied
ERROR [{service}] TimeoutInterceptor: Request duration {timeout}s matches configured deadline exactly — this is a hard timeout hit, not a network issue | {downstream} did not respond within budget

ERROR [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Thread acquisition wait exceeded {timeout}s | caller abandoned | pool state: {active} running, {waiting} waiting, {thread_max} max | reduce {downstream} timeout to fix

ERROR [{service}] CircuitBreaker[{downstream}]: Timeout error rate reached {error_pct}% over 60s window — tripping to OPEN | note: slow requests not counted until timeout fires — breaker response lagged saturation by {timeout}s
WARN [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Thread utilisation {timeout_pct}% ({active}/{thread_max}) | average hold time {latency}ms (baseline <200ms) | cause: blocked on {downstream} I/O
WARN [{downstream}] ConnPool: {conn_count} of {thread_max} connections to {deep_downstream} open >{downstream_timeout}s without completing a response | approaching pool ceiling
WARN [{service}] LatencyBudget: P90 latency for outbound calls to {downstream} is {latency}ms, consuming {timeout_pct}% of total request budget | insufficient headroom for response serialisation
WARN [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Queue depth {waiting} — estimated wait {timeout}s | consider reducing timeout on {downstream} calls to free threads sooner | current config: {timeout}s

WARN [{downstream}] SlowLog: {affected_count} calls to {deep_downstream} exceeded 1s in last minute | if latency reaches {downstream_timeout}s, upstream callers will begin accumulating blocked threads

WARN [{service}] TimeoutConfig: {service} timeout for {downstream} ({timeout}s) exceeds {downstream} timeout for {deep_downstream} ({downstream_timeout}s) | gap allows pool saturation before downstream fails fast

WARN [{service}] ActiveConnections: {conn_count} active HTTP connections to {downstream} with last-byte time >5s | connections are live (TCP keepalive confirmed) but {downstream} not flushing response

WARN [{downstream}] BacklogMonitor: {waiting} incoming requests from {service} queued behind {conn_count} in-flight calls to {deep_downstream} | if {deep_downstream} does not recover, all queued requests will timeout

WARN [{service}] GracefulDegradation: fallback cache miss rate 100% — all requests require live {downstream} data | no degraded mode available | timeout propagation will result in full service unavailability

WARN [{service}] ThreadDumpTrigger: {active} threads in WAITING state, all blocked in SocketInputStream.read() | stack trace points to {downstream} HTTP client read | timeout fires in {timeout}s if no response
WARN [{deep_downstream}] QueryMonitor: {affected_count} queries running >5s | upstream read timeouts: {downstream}={downstream_timeout}s, {service}={timeout}s — both at risk of saturation

WARN [{service}] NetworkMonitor: RTT to {downstream} is {latency}ms (normal <20ms) | elevated RTT not caused by network packet loss — {downstream} is processing slowly, likely blocked on {deep_downstream}

INFO [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Utilisation returning to normal — {active} active threads (was {thread_max}) | {downstream} response time recovering after {deep_downstream} query optimisation deployed

INFO [{downstream}] TimeoutConfig: Read timeout for {deep_downstream} reduced from {downstream_timeout}s to 2s | existing in-flight requests still expire at {downstream_timeout}s | new requests will fast-fail
INFO [{service}] TraceId={trace_id}: Request completed in {latency}ms | call to {downstream} accounted for {timeout_pct}% of total latency | stored for baseline comparison
INFO [{service}] CircuitBreaker[{downstream}]: State changed OPEN → HALF_OPEN | allowing {rate} probe requests | pool {pool_name} utilisation: {active}/{thread_max}

INFO [{service}] IncidentTimeline: {deep_downstream} slowdown T+0s | {downstream} pool saturated T+{downstream_timeout}s | {service} pool saturated T+{timeout}s | edge timeout T+{total_timeout}s | propagation chain confirmed

INFO [{service}] Remediation: {service} timeout for {downstream} reduced from {timeout}s to {downstream_timeout}s | ensures {service} threads released before {downstream} exhausts | breaking the hold chain
INFO [{service}] pool={pool_name}: Steady state — {active} active, 0 queued, {thread_max} max | average hold time {latency}ms | incident resolved | monitoring for recurrence
